data.title: Travel + Leisure: Positive Momentum Continues
data.description:
Shares of Travel + Leisure (TNL) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining nearly 40%, and investors received more good news Wednesday when the company reported solid quarterly results. The recurring revenue nature of the timeshare business has insulated it from pressures in travel spending, while its high-quality customer base has made credit quality comparatively resilient. I last covered TNL in November, reiterating the stock as a “Buy,” and since then it has gained a further 22%, justifying my bullishness. With shares past my $70 price target and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit TNL to see if the good news is now fully priced in.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Travel + Leisure earned $1.83 per share, which beat estimates by a penny as revenue grew 6% to $1.03 billion. Adjusted EBITDA was up 8% to $272 million, thanks to ongoing margin gains from the healthy utilization of its resorts. Additionally, TNL is undertaking a “resort optimization initiative” where it will exit underperforming or more seasonal resorts with newer resorts and locations. This resulted in a one-time charge of $216 million in Q4, though there should be ~$20 million of run-rate benefits to EBITDA going forward given reduced costs.

VOI business drives growth

Within its vacation ownership business, revenue grew 8% to $875 million, driving a 14% increase in EBITDA to $252 million. Sales of vacation ownership interest, or “VOI,” (this is the term the industry uses for timeshares), were up 8% to $638 million. It had 5% more tour attendees, and its volume per guest (“VPG”) was up 2%. These are both healthy metrics. More customers taking tours speaks to growing demand, and a higher conversion of these guests into sales speaks to a strong marketing and product offering.

TNL continues to benefit from having one of the higher-quality customer bases, helping to largely insulate it from affordability pressures on discretionary spending, which have limited results at other travel companies. Its average VOI owner has an income over $100k, and the FICO score of new buyers is solidly prime, having moved above 740. During 2025, it added more loan provisions, ending the year at 20.7%, up from 19.3% a year ago. Delinquencies are within expectations, and I expect this to continue, given my view that a recession is unlikely in 2026. With stable credit trends, this should migrate back towards 20% in 2026, providing some incremental free cash flow. Given the underlying credit quality of its owners, I am comfortable with existing loss reserves.

Aside from VOI sales, property management revenue was up 6% to $222 million, while financing revenue was up 1% to $116 million. With a growing membership and strong sales activity, we should see these revenue streams continue to provide revenue growth, and I expect mid-to-upper single-digit growth from the vacation ownership segment in 2026.

Its travel and management segment continues to face some pressures, with revenue down 6% to $148 million. As a result, EBITDA was down 10% to $47 million, and this exchange business has been struggling for some time. While transactions were up 3%, revenue per transaction fell by 9% due to an ongoing mix shift towards travel club transactions. I see this business facing long-term pressure as consumers continue to move to the newer, more flexible VOI model. Still, it will continue to provide free cash flow for some time. Whereas its vacation ownership segment drives growth, this is a legacy cash cow operation.

Cash flow enables ongoing capital returns

During 2025, it generated $516 million of free cash flow. Importantly, the company has a solid balance sheet with $3.5 billion of corporate debt and $253 million of cash (note that this debt excludes the $2.1 billion of nonrecourse securitizations that support its VOI receivables). Net leverage is 3.1x, a reasonable place for a business with recurring cash flow to operate.

As a result, it can return free cash flow to investors. Last year, TNL returned $449 million to shareholders via dividends and buybacks. Its share count is down 7.4% over the past year thanks to these repurchases. Alongside results, TNL announced a new $750 million buyback program. Plus, it plans to raise its quarterly dividend to $0.60, giving shares a 3.2% forward yield. As you can see, TNL has aggressively returned capital to investors during its time as a public company.

Outlook points to further upside

Looking forward, TNL expects to generate $1.03-$1.055 billion of EBITDA in 2026, up over 5% from last year. Underpinning this, it expects to grow VOI sales by 1%-5%; however, we are likely to see a small decline in VPG. TNL is making a concerted effort to boost sales to new owners (vs. existing owners seeking to upgrade). First-time buyers tend to buy lower-cost packages, but this can create a pipeline of future sales as they seek to upgrade over time.

Back in November, I was targeting ~$5 of run-rate capital returns per share. With the ongoing strength in sales and quality of its loan book, this view feels increasingly conservative. I see TNL positioned to return about $400 million of cash to investors, especially with leverage nearing 3.0x. This points to closer to ~$6 of capital returns. Given the rally in shares, we are unlikely to see the share count fall quite as dramatically, but a ~5% reduction from buybacks is well within the realm of possibility.

Shares remain a "Buy"

At ~$75, shares are now trading at an 8% capital return yield. Even after their large run, shares are still not that expensive. That reflects just how cheap TNL was trading a few months ago when there was excessive fear about credit losses on its receivables portfolio. While funded with nonrecourse debt, worse performance can limit how much residual value is left for TNL in these structures. Given its best-in-breed customer base and healthy balance sheet, we could see TNL move towards a 7% free cash flow, or $85, though I view this as the upper limit of valuation.

After this solid quarter and outlook, I see no rush to exit TNL, especially with a growing dividend and benefit from repurchase activity. I expect shares to migrate to $80-$85 over the next year. Combined with its dividend, that leaves sufficient upside to remain a “Buy.” While I believe the majority of the upside has occurred, TNL can continue to deliver strong performance in 2026.

data.title: KLA Corporation: I Would Take Some Profits (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in early September 2025, I reiterated my hold rating on KLA Corporation (KLAC). Despite overall resilient results and robust cash flows, there was notable top line growth deceleration in their segments. Forward-looking guidance seemed disappointing, and I concluded that the valuation was around fair. In hindsight, I was probably too conservative on KLA stock, as the chart below clearly shows that there have been monstrous gains since the publication of my previous update. Regardless, today I hope to offer a new in-depth analysis for readers and see if my rating needs any revising.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Q2 was a mixed quarter for KLA. Top-line growth slowed despite memory tailwinds, but margins showed expansion. Moving forward, guidance for Q3 is also mixed, and the company's exposure to China remains a very real risk to their business. With the forward P/E very near multiyear highs, there is room for the valuation to correct. I believe investors should now take some profits even if they aren't exiting, and so I'm downgrading KLA stock to a sell.

Growth Continues To Moderate

While the stock has advanced mightily, KLA's top line growth seems to be headed in the opposite direction. For FY2026 Q2, the company posted total revenues worth $3.3 billion, up 7.12% YoY. While this is still quite a respectable increase, you can see above that this represents the third consecutive quarter of deceleration. Therefore, the business as a whole has become a bit sluggish in my view.

Given the unrelenting demand for semiconductors and the stock's performance in recent months, I honestly expected more from their top-line numbers. Analysts would disagree, as they actually still beat expectations by $46.47 million. Nonetheless, first impressions are that momentum seems a bit weak.

Memory Is A Key Driver

In the year-ago quarter, memory made up just 24% of KLA's semiconductor process control segment's revenue. As you can see on the right, the importance of memory has increased significantly in the past year, as it now makes up 40% of the segment's revenue. By now, it is no secret that the tech sector is facing a memory chip supply crunch as AI data centers have created unprecedented demand. Many sectors, like automotive and consumer electronics, could feel the effects, and so it's quite intuitive that chip manufacturers are looking to up production.

For KLA, that is clearly a tailwind, and the increasing revenue contribution from their memory business is a good indication of this. For the semiconductor industry as a whole, in 2025, logic product sales growth of 39.9% outpaced memory product growth of 34.8%. Moving forward, I would say memory chips will be more important than ever, as total semiconductor sales are projected to reach $1 trillion in 2026. I expect memory to continue being a leading driver for KLA's growth, and contribution to revenue is likely to continue to rise as a result of these tailwinds.

China Still A Key Risk

In the introduction, I mentioned that KLA has decreased their reliance on China. That was the case in their Q2 results as well. In the prior year period, 36% of their revenue came from the Chinese market, while for FY2026 Q2, 30% of revenues came from the world's second largest economy. While the reduced reliance definitely helps with their business' geopolitical risk, I would say that they are still at the mercy of such headwinds should they arise.

At the end of December, it was reported that Chinese regulators are mandating chipmakers to use 50% domestic equipment. For a company like KLA that has significant exposure to China, I would say that these developments are well worth monitoring for investors. They're growing less reliant, but if U.S. - China relations sour in the near future, KLA may still be in for a blow that can't be easily mitigated.

Margins & Bottom Line Performance

While top-line growth has moved in an unfavorable direction, the company's margins have expanded to soften the blow. For adjusted gross margin, there has been YoY expansion from 61.7% to 62.6% as a sign that their pricing power remains solid and that cost controls are strong.

As for operating margin, there was a 50 basis point expansion to 42.8%. Since this improvement is smaller than the gross margin improvement, there may have been some slight expense control pressures, but nonetheless, expansion in both gross and operating margin can't really be complained about.

At the end of the day, their adjusted EPS of $8.85 represented 7.93% growth. This bested the top line growth rate and analyst expectations, and so profitability for KLA is not something worth worrying about right now based on these results.

Do Capital Returns Signal Caution?

One of the financial highlights for KLA in Q2 was their FCF growth. As you can see above, they reported FCF of $1.26 billion, and that represents an impressive growth rate of 66.67% YoY. This is a multiyear high by a good margin. Furthermore, FCF margin improved from 25% to 38% YoY, while FCF conversion improved from 69% to 108%. These are all signals that cash generation by their business has been rock solid. However, the company doesn't seem overly eager to share the cash with shareholders.

While FCF increased sharply YoY, share repurchases for the quarter dropped from $650 million to $548 million even as total dividend payout increased from $227 million to $250 million. As a whole, capital returns are down YoY, and could that be a signal of cautiousness? Maybe. The fact that they chose to reduce stock buybacks may signal an unattractive valuation, and so investors should keep that in mind.

Quarterly Outlook

For their FY2026 Q3, they expect revenues to be at around $3.35 billion, and this was above analyst consensus. That implies a growth rate of 9.5% YoY and would snap the run of consecutive top-line growth decelerations. Therefore, there are signs of a reaccelerating business. That was the main point of weakness in Q2, and it seems they'll see their fortunes improve.

For gross margin, they are expecting 61.75% at the midpoint. This would actually be an unfavorable change from the year-ago quarter's 63%, and so the cost control strength from Q2 won't continue. Lastly, for adjusted EPS, their $9.08 midpoint would be an 8.0% increase, and this was also above consensus. This growth rate would be nearly unchanged from Q2.

As a whole, I would say that guidance is a little mixed. Q2 results were mixed, with growth slowing but margins expanding. Funny enough, for Q3, growth is expected to reaccelerate, but margin may be under pressure. At the end of the day, the bottom line growth will be nearly unchanged, and so I conclude that the outlook is respectable but also some distance from being perfect.

Valuation Seems Rich

Despite retreating a bit from the high recently, KLA's forward P/E ratio is still at one of the highest levels in the past few years. I believe the valuation may be a bit rich given the fundamentals we have discussed today.

In this analysis, it was shown that the company's opportunity in memory is large, but there are some challenges moving forward. As stated above, EPS growth is expected to remain in just the single digits, with the company likely to see margin contraction moving forward. Furthermore, the possibly largest risk to KLA's business is their still significant exposure to the Chinese market. The regulatory environment there is growing a bit hostile and can be unpredictable.

Given their opportunity to continue benefitting from AI tailwinds but also the notable challenges and risks the company is facing, I would say that a forward P/E near multiyear highs is a little bit on the optimistic side of things. In my view, there is room for a pullback in the valuation, as there may be some memory-related froth here.

Conclusion

At the current valuation, I would say that KLA has quite a bit of good news priced in. If everything goes to plan and risks don't materialize into headwinds, the stock could perform just fine. However, when considering the distribution of outcomes, I would say there are reasons to be a bit cautious at current levels. The stock price strongly captures the opportunity in AI, particularly in memory, but may have overlooked some other less bullish factors. At this point, I believe investors would be wise to partially take profits, and so I have decided to downgrade KLA to a sell rating.

data.title: Janus Henderson Securitized Income ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 1.62% and the ICE BofA US ABS & CMBS TR returned 1.21%.

Contributors/detractors

Security selection within agency collateralized mortgage obligations ('CMO') and commercial mortgage-backed securities ('CMBS') contributed. Exposure to agency MBS and asset-backed securities ('ABS') detracted.

Outlook

A resilient economy, AI-driven investment, and anticipated Fed easing create a favorable backdrop for securitized fixed income. Active strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.

Investment environment

  • U.S. securitized markets delivered positive returns for the quarter. Short-term Treasury yields rallied, helping to drive performance. Securitized sectors broadly outperformed investment-grade ('IG') corporates and U.S. Treasuries.
  • In December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by 25 basis points (BPS) for a third consecutive meeting in response to a weaker jobs market, with the U.S. unemployment rate hitting a four-year high of 4.6% in November. Other data releases were more positive, with inflation dipping below expectations in November and third-quarter gross domestic product ('GDP') growth that surprised on the upside.
  • Speculation continued to build around who would replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell when his term expires in May and the degree to which their appointment might signal a shift in the central bank's traditional operational independence.
  • Spreads on IG ABS ended the quarter three basis points wider, at 52 bps, while spreads on IG CMBS ended unchanged, at 81 bps.

Portfolio review

Credit spreads continued to trade tight as investor sentiment remained upbeat amid strong corporate profitability, expected Fed easing, and robust economic growth supported by tax cuts and heavy investment in artificial intelligence ('AI'). While securitized spreads are trading around their cyclical tight levels, we believe securitized assets still are attractively priced relative to corporates as interest-rate volatility cools and consumer fundamentals remain strong.

Security selection contributed positively due, in part, to the fund's CMBS and agency CMO picks, but also thanks to the fund's greater diversification across securitized sectors compared with the benchmark. The fund's exposure to mortgages was particularly helpful to performance, as the mortgage sector ended the year on a high, helped by a steeper yield curve and lower interest rate volatility. Security selection within ABS detracted.

During the quarter, we increased our allocation to CLOs and MBS while trimming our exposure to CMBS. We took an interest, too, in data center-backed ABS, in line with the broader AI trend.

We continue to prefer the yield exposure at the short end of the yield curve, where investors may be better shielded from increases in term premiums on the long end due to potential concerns surrounding Fed independence, fiscal discipline, and foreign demand for U.S. Treasuries. We believe this view is further supported by the combination of forthcoming tax cuts, stubborn inflation, and upward revisions to economic growth estimates.

Manager outlook

While 2025 was an exceptional year for fixed income, we are similarly constructive on the outlook for 2026, albeit with a focus on earning carry rather than pursuing significant spread compression. In our view, current yield levels continue to present an attractive entry point for securitized fixed income strategies, supported by a constructive macroeconomic backdrop and favorable monetary policy trends.

U.S. economic growth is expected to remain positive, with real GDP projected at approximately 2.0% in 2026. While some sectors exhibit late-cycle characteristics, resilient consumer spending and corporate profitability – bolstered by AI adoption – should sustain economic momentum. Significant AI-driven investments in chips and data centers are anticipated to further fuel growth.

We think risks surrounding tariffs, immigration reform, and geopolitics are expected to moderate, while tax reforms under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act ('OBIBBA') may provide additional tailwinds. Inflation, despite disinflationary progress in energy and housing, is likely to remain above the Fed's target, in the 2.6% to 3.0% range, due to sticky services prices and delayed tariff effects.

Fiscal deficits and concerns over Fed independence may keep long-end yields rangebound. While uncertainty surrounds the Fed's near-term decisions, markets anticipate two to three rate cuts by year-end 2026. The Fed's pivot from quantitative tightening to quantitative easing – and its readiness to ease aggressively if growth falters – should support spreads. AI-driven productivity gains and tighter immigration policies could create a unique dynamic of strong growth alongside weaker labor markets, reinforcing the case for continued Fed easing.

Broadly speaking, key themes within the portfolio include our bullishness on credit spread carry amid robust corporate and consumer fundamentals and expected Fed cuts. Securitized assets are also attractively valued compared with corporates and offer a relatively stronger credit-quality mix. Furthermore, real yields are still above 1% and offer inflation-adjusted return potential.

A resilient economy, AI-driven investment, and anticipated Fed easing create a favorable backdrop for securitized fixed income in 2026. Active, diversified strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.

Portfolio management

John Kerschner, CFA

Nick Childs, CFA

Definitions

10-year Treasury yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature 10 years from the date of purchase.

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Carry is the excess income earned from holding a higher-yielding security relative to another.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

Duration measures a bond price's sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond's duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa.

Quantitative Easing ('QE') is a government monetary policy occasionally used to increase the money supply by buying government securities or other securities from the market.

Quantitative Tightening ('QT') is a government monetary policy occasionally used to decrease the money supply by either selling government securities or letting them mature and removing them from its cash balances.

Volatility measures risk using the dispersion of returns for a given investment.

A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

The opinions are as of 12/31/25, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Holdings are subject to change without notice.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

The Fund will typically enter into "to be announced" or "TBA" commitments when purchasing MBS, which allows the Fund to agree to pay for certain yet-to-be issued securities at a future date and which may have a leveraging effect on the Fund.

Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, inflation, credit and default risk. The bond market is volatile. As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if an issuer fails to make timely payments or its credit strength weakens.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOS) are debt securities issued in different tranches, with varying degrees of risk, and backed by an underlying portfolio consisting primarily of below investment grade corporate loans. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if payments are not made timely or credit strength weakens. CLOs are subject to liquidity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk, call risk and the risk of default of the underlying assets.

Derivatives can be more volatile and sensitive to economic or market changes than other investments, which could result in losses exceeding the original investment and magnified by leverage.

Increased portfolio turnover may result in higher expenses and potentially higher net taxable gains or losses.

Actively managed portfolios may fail to produce the intended results. No investment strategy can ensure a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Securitized products, such as mortgage- and asset-backed securities, are more sensitive to interest rate changes, have extension and prepayment risk, and are subject to more credit, valuation and liquidity risk than other fixed-income securities.

Index performance does not reflect the expenses of managing a portfolio as an index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

data.title: Tracking Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Berkshire Hathaway’s 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Warren Buffett’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/17/2026. Please visit our Tracking 10 Years Of Berkshire Hathaway's Investment Portfolio article series for an idea of how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the moves during Q3 2025.

During Q4 2025, Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.A) (BRK.B) 13F stock portfolio value increased from ~$267B to ~$274B. The top five positions account for ~70% of the portfolio: Apple Inc., American Express, Bank of America, Coca-Cola, and Chevron. There are 40 individual stock positions, many of which are small compared to the overall size of the portfolio.

Warren Buffett’s writings (PDFs) are a treasure trove of information and are a very good source for anyone starting out on individual investing. So are the classics “The Intelligent Investor” and “Security Analysis.”

Note 1: It was disclosed in August 2020 that Berkshire had built ~5% stakes in five Japanese trading businesses: ITOCHU, Marubeni, Mitsubishi, Mitsui, and Sumitomo. The stakes increased to over 10% each as of Q1 2025. The 2021 AR had the following regarding the initial purchases in three of them: 5.6% ownership stake in ITOCHU Corporation (ITOCF) at a cost basis of $4.70, 5.5% ownership stake in Mitsubishi Corporation (MSBHF) at a cost basis of $8.57, and 5.7% ownership stake in Mitsui & Co. (MITSF) at a cost basis of $8.65. ITOCF, MSBHF, and MITSF currently trade at ~$15, ~$32, and ~$36, respectively. The cost basis is adjusted to reflect stock splits.

Note 2: Berkshire Hathaway fully exited their stake in BYD Company (BYDDY) by September 2025. Their ownership stake was ~20% as of Q3 2022. The stake fell below the 5% ownership threshold during Q2 2024.

New Stakes:

New York Times (NYT): NYT is 0.13% of the portfolio position established this quarter at prices between ~$54 and ~$71. The stock currently trades above that range at $74.03.

Stake Increases:

Chevron Corporation (CVX): CVX is a large (top five) 7.24% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2020 at prices between ~$72 and ~$91. Q1 2021 saw a ~50% selling at prices between ~$85 and ~$112, while in Q3 2021 there was a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$94 and ~$106. That was followed with another one-third increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$102 and ~$119. Q1 2022 saw a whopping ~315% stake increase at prices between ~$119 and ~$171. The three quarters through Q3 2023 saw one-third selling at prices between ~$150 and ~$188. There was a ~14% stake increase during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$142 and ~$169. The stock currently trades at ~$181. H1 2024 saw a ~6% trimming, while during Q2 2025 there was a ~3% increase. This quarter also saw a ~7% stake increase.

Note 1: Berkshire has a ~6.2% ownership stake in the business.

Note 2: Berkshire avoided disclosing this stake in the Q3 2020 13F filing by making use of the section 13(f) Confidential Treatment Requests. An amendment filed later disclosed the activity.

Chubb Limited (CB): CB is 3.90% of the portfolio position purchased during H2 2023 at prices between ~$188 and ~$230. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$225 and ~$259. That was followed by a ~16% further increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$264 and ~$291. This quarter also saw a ~9% further increase at prices between ~$265 and ~$315. The stock currently trades at ~$332. They are building this position.

Note: Berkshire avoided disclosing this stake in the Q3 2023 13F filing by making use of the section 13(f) Confidential Treatment Requests. They have an ~8.5% ownership stake in the business.

Domino’s Pizza (DPZ): DPZ is 0.51% of the portfolio position purchased during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$402 and ~$504. There was an ~87% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$407 and ~$475. Q1 2025 saw a ~10% further increase at prices between ~$397 and ~$501. That was followed by a ~13% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$421 and ~$496. This quarter also saw a ~12% stake increase at prices between ~$393 and ~$442. The stock currently trades below their purchase price ranges at ~$374. They are continuing to build this position.

Note: They have a 9.9% ownership stake in the business.

Lamar Advertising (LAMR): The very small 0.06% LAMR stake was established during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$100 and ~$123, and it is now above that range at ~$133. There was a minor ~3% stake increase during the last quarter and a marginal increase this quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Apple Inc. (AAPL): AAPL is currently the largest 13F portfolio stake by far at ~23%. It was built between Q1 2016 and Q1 2018 at a cost basis of ~$35 per share. The stake was decreased by 13% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$169 and ~$195. The next quarter saw another ~49% selling at prices between ~$165 and ~$216. That was followed by a ~25% reduction during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$207 and ~$234. Q2 2025 also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$169 and ~$225. That was followed by a ~15% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$202 and ~$258. The stock currently trades at ~$264. This quarter also saw a ~4% trimming.

Bank of America (BAC): Berkshire established this large (top three) ~10% of the portfolio position through the exercise of Bank of America warrants. The warrants had a strike price of $7.14. The cost to exercise was $5B, and it was funded using the $5B in 6% preferred stock they held. There was a ~30% stake increase in Q3 2018 at prices between $27.75 and $31.80. Q3 2024 saw ~23% selling at prices between $36.41 and $43.84. That was followed by a ~15% reduction during the next quarter at prices between $39 and $47.51. Q1 2025 also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$39 and ~$48. That was followed by a ~4% trimming during the next quarter at prices between ~$33 and ~$48. The last quarter also saw a ~6% trimming at prices between ~$45 and ~$53. There was a ~9% further reduction this quarter at prices between ~$48.50 and ~$56.50. The stock currently trades at $52.74. They are harvesting gains.

Note: Berkshire’s overall cost basis is ~$14, and ownership stake is at ~6.8%.

DaVita Inc. (DVA): DVA is 1.32% of the portfolio position that was aggressively built over several quarters in the 2012-13 timeframe at prices between $30 and $49. The stock currently trades at ~$150 compared to Berkshire’s overall cost basis of ~$45 per share. Q3 2020 saw ~5% (2M shares) selling at $88 per share. American Express (AXP) and Coca-Cola (KO): These two very large stakes were kept steady during the last ~10 years. Buffett has said these positions will be held permanently. data.title: Lord Abbett Floating Rate Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. fixed income markets finished the year on a strong note, registering solid returns across various sectors. The bullish narrative surrounding markets centered on additional easing from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) and a resilient macroeconomic and consumer backdrop. The Fed delivered an additional 50 basis points (bps) of rate cuts during the quarter, including a 25 bp December cut that occurred despite market odds falling sharply late in November. Real GDP increased a better-than-expected +4.3% annualized in Q3, with consumer spending acting as a strong catalyst for growth. Other tailwinds included better-than-expected corporate earnings and revision trends, sustained hyperscaler investment supporting the AI theme, and an improvement in trade dynamics. However, several headwinds, including signs of labor market softening and consumer affordability pressures, housing weakness, data uncertainty from the government shutdown, and relatively stretched investor sentiment, partially offset these positive themes.1

  • U.S. leveraged loans exhibited solid returns over the quarter, gaining +1.22%.2 Performance was primarily driven signs of a resilient U.S. economy, thin liquidity, an active primary, and a more dovish Fed outcome. Within the sector, higher-rated tiers outperformed, as BBs outgained both Bs and CCCs. Looking at sectors, Retail and Transportation led the way, while Chemical and Housing sectors lagged.3 Gross issuance was also solid over the quarter with over $171bn in volume, led by repricing and refinancing activity. Default/LME activity in the loan index declined; including distressed exchanges, the par-weighted U.S. loan default rate finished the fourth quarter at approximately 2.87%, more than 40 bps lower than the prior period.3

Fund Review

  • The Fund returned +1.75%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested for the quarter ended December 31, 2025. The Fund's benchmark, the Morningstar LSTA U.S. Leveraged Loan Index*, returned +1.22% during the same period.

Past performance does not guarantee future returns.

  • Over the period, the Fund outperformed its benchmark, driven by credit selection within both Single B and CCC rating tiers. Specifically, within CCCs, the Fund was overweight/void several positions within the index that underperformed, which ultimately contributed to relative performance. Credit selection within several sectors was also a contributor, driven by select overweights within Materials that outperformed, as well as underweights to certain exposure within Automobiles that lagged the benchmark.
  • Several allocations detracted from relative returns over the quarter, specifically when looking at ratings. While selection within CCCs was a positive contributor, the Fund's overweight in CCCs and underweight to BBs detracted overall given better returns in higher-rated tiers in the benchmark. Within sectors, selection within Consumer Durables and Apparel as well as Consumer Services (ie Gaming) were primary detractors.

Fund Positioning & Outlook

  • Continue to target loans with high carry.

Loan spreads have remained relatively tight, and a high percentage of the loan index now trades above par, limiting opportunities to add convexity to the Fund. In this context, we targeted loan positions that offered higher levels of carry. We believe that loans remain an attractive source of income as yields remain elevated, particularly over yields of high yield corporate bonds, which provides more income and additional cushion if spreads widen.

  • We maintained barbell approach for credit rating exposure.

We continued to position the Fund with a modest overweight to BBs and CCCs and underweight to Single Bs. For the quarter, we increased the Fund's allocation to CCCs where we found opportunities that offered more attractive entry points. We focused on higher exposure to shorter-dated securities where the performance is likely to be catalyst-driven. We trimmed select BB and Single B positions over the period, mainly in Energy and Healthcare Equipment sectors, where we felt valuations were relatively tight.

  • Sector exposure remains relatively balanced.

The Fund's top overweight positions remained in the Utilities and Food, Beverage and Tobacco sectors, with primary additions to Materials and Telecommunications Services sectors. We continued to focus on more idiosyncratic positions within these sectors and also are wary of certain AI disruption themes that have been recently more prominent in the loan market. On the other hand, we reduced Consumer Services, which is now a top underweight, and as well as Healthcare Equipment.

  • We remain constructive on the bank loan market amid a favorable economic backdrop.

Credit markets exhibited a strong year of returns in 2025, and we expect this trend to continue as we enter 2026. We remain constructive on the overall macroeconomic landscape in the U.S., supported by the continued easing cycle of the Fed, with several rate cuts priced in for the year, declining inflation and resilience in consumer spending, which we believe should be supportive of risk-assets. Loan issuers also continue to be supported by solid fundamentals, which were solid through the fastest Fed hiking cycle in over 40 years. And following multiple rate cuts by the Fed from peak level in 2024, we expect underlying credit metrics of loan issuers, such as interest and fixed charge coverage ratios, to steadily improve. We also continue to see a supportive technical, particularly on the demand side with continued demand from CLO creation. However, we are wary of several potential headwinds such as more rapid softness in macroeconomic and employment data as well as uncertainties around Artificial Intelligence (AI) and potential disruption within legacy sectors in the universe.

data.title: CSPF: Attractive New Active Preferred Equity ETF From Cohen
data.description:
Thesis

Cohen & Steers (CNS) is a global investment management firm that specializes in REITs and preferred shares. There are a number of ETFs and CEFs outstanding from this name, some of which we have covered before. In today's article we are going to talk about a new ETF from the manager, namely the Cohen & Steers Preferred & Income Opptys Actv ETF (CSPF).

New fund in the ETF structure

CSPF came to market in early 2025 and is now completing its first year of existence. The main objective for the fund is total return, but a major part of the strategy revolves around investing in institutional preferred securities. These are high-denomination securities (often $1,000 par value) that offer better terms and higher credit quality than the $25 par value preferred stocks available to the general public:

As we can see from the above graph from Nuveen, $1,000 par value securities tend to be 'cheaper' than the widely available $25 par ones.

Cohen has a very deep expertise in the preferred space, with a large AUM dedicated to the asset class. Some of the analytics for the new fund are as follows:

  • AUM: $128 M
  • Expense ratio: 0.59%
  • Portfolio duration: 4.6 years
  • SEC yield: 5.09%
  • Fund type: Active

The presented expense ratio is actually a net one, with the manager having the lower fee until June 2026, when it moves to 0.75%. The expense ratio is on the high side, but this is an active fund; thus, higher fees are to be expected. As per the Seeking Alpha platform the median expense ratio is 0.5%:

We do not mind high fees as long as the performance justifies them, and we will have a look at that as well (keep in mind that presented total returns are inclusive of expense ratios). As we shall see in the 'Performance' section, the ETF outperformed its peers, thus validating its active approach and management.

Current composition

Let us now look at how the fund is currently set-up:

The name is overweight banking preferred equities, which make up 43% of the portfolio, followed by utilities at 13.5% and energy at 11%. This is not unusual, with the vast majority of preferred shares coming from this sector. Kindly note the ETF can have (and does have) allocations to CoCo's:

Contingent convertible bonds (CoCo bonds) are high-yield, perpetual hybrid debt instruments issued primarily by European banks to bolster capital, introduced post-2008 to prevent bailouts. They function as debt (paying coupons) but automatically convert into equity or are written down if a bank's capital falls below a specific regulatory threshold.

CoCos are arguably higher risk securities, and not all ETFs contain them. CSPF does have an allocation, though:

Roughly 34% of the holdings are CoCos, meaning the main risk here for the fund is a severe recession led by financials. Keep in mind what happened with the Credit Suisse CoCos:

In March 2023, Swiss regulators mandated a $17 billion write-down of Credit Suisse Group AG's Additional Tier 1 (AT1) CoCo bonds to zero as part of its emergency takeover by UBS Group AG. This controversial move, where bondholders were wiped out while shareholders retained some value, caused major market volatility, though legal challenges in 2025 regarding the legality of the write-down are ongoing.

Also note the fund tends to focus on over-the-counter ('OTC') securities, which represent 55% of the ETF when excluding CoCos. This simply means the ETF reaches an institutional market that retail investors do not readily have access to.

The fund portfolio is diversified, with the U.S. and Europe as the main geographic exposures:

The fund composition is overweight fixed to floating securities:

In the preferred securities market, names are usually underwritten with a five-year non-call period and a fixed-to-floating reset (the reset nowadays can be into constant maturity treasuries plus a spread rather than SOFR plus a spread).

When looking at the fund portfolio, there is nothing unusual here, with an overweight in institutional preferred shares, but otherwise a typical composition. What makes a big difference here is the active stance of the fund, and the manager's expertise in the space. Let us see how it has fared since its inception.

Fund performance

Since the fund is fairly new, we only have one year of data:

The name has done very well in the past year when compared to a cohort formed by Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA), the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) and the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Securities Fund (FFC).

All names outside of FFC are ETFs, and FFC has outperformed only due to its leverage. More importantly, though, CSPF had a very narrow drawdown during the April 2025 market risk-off event, which speaks very well to the security selection by the manager. Risk analytics are not available yet on the Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab, but they will be lower than the cohort when it comes to volatility. The ETF is active; thus, the manager has the ability to underwrite those issuances that have the best risk/reward metrics.

Macro outlook

We are starting 2026 with a Federal Reserve that is set to cut rates, thus helping fixed income assets. CSPF comes with a 4.6-year duration, thus is set to gain as rates move lower. Furthermore, this monetary policy stance keeps short-term rates elevated but supports a steeper yield curve, benefiting bank net interest margins and overall sector health.

In the context of very tight high-yield credit spreads, bank preferred equity offers superior risk/reward metrics with higher credit quality and robust CET 1 ratios. Also, limited new issuance from banks driven by a changing regulatory landscape creates scarcity, thus supporting pricing and potentially compressing spreads further.

Risk factors

With a large CoCo sleeve, the ETF is prone to significant downside if we were to have a recession that greatly impacted bank balance sheets. As we saw with Credit Suisse, CoCo bonds can move to zero fairly fast if there is significant capital erosion with a financial institution.

The second risk factor for this ETF is made up of tight credit spreads in the preferred space. Just like the larger high yield universe, preferred shares have seen spreads compress in the past year, thus leaving significant downside in a market sell-off.

Conclusion

CSPF is a fixed-income ETF from Cohen. The name focuses on $1,000 par value preferred shares, and has total return as an objective. This is an active ETF that charges an expense ratio on the high side but has delivered in the past year versus a cohort of peers. The name represents a great diversification tool and an apt choice for a portfolio in the preferred shares space.

data.title: France's Very Low Inflation Rate Is A Major Challenge For Public Finances
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

French inflation persistently undershooting the eurozone’s average

In France, inflation stood at 0.3% year-on-year in January, down from 0.8% in December. According to the harmonised index, it reached 0.4%, compared with 0.7% the previous month. Such a low level had not been observed since the Covid-19 pandemic, a period marked by the collapse in demand linked to lockdowns. Before that, one would have to go back to 2016.

This weakness is primarily explained by the sharp fall in prices of manufactured goods (‑1.2% year-on-year), linked to a change in the seasonal sales timing.

Energy prices are also weighing heavily on inflation, with a year-on-year decline of 7.6%, reflecting both developments in global markets and the 15% cut in regulated gas tariffs decided in February 2025. By contrast, food inflation accelerated in January to 1.9% year-on-year, while services inflation slowed to 1.7%. Core inflation stands at just 0.7%.

The French situation contrasts sharply with that of the rest of the euro area, where inflation reached an average of 1.7% in January, according to the flash estimate, and where core inflation remains much higher, at 2.3%.

In this context, criticism is growing in France of an ECB monetary policy deemed ill-suited to the national situation. With the key interest rate held at 2% for several months, some argue that the French economy is being subjected to an excessively restrictive policy. This issue was once again raised by the Governor of the Banque de France, François Villeroy de Galhau, this Wednesday before the National Assembly.

Is monetary policy too restrictive for France?

It is true that with very low inflation and policy rates at 2%, the real interest rate faced by the French economy is positive and higher than in neighbouring countries where inflation is more dynamic.

Some people argue that this weighs on investment and, more broadly, on economic activity. However, in assessing how restrictive monetary policy is, it is not inflation in a single month that matters, but rather expected inflation and its trajectory over the coming years.

Inflation is expected to rise in France in the months ahead. The timing of seasonal sales differs in 2026 compared with 2025, which will lead to a more pronounced increase in prices in February. In addition, the 15% cut in electricity prices implemented on 1 February 2025 will drop out of the base effect this month, mechanically pushing energy inflation higher.

This rebound will nevertheless remain limited, and my forecast of average inflation of 1.3% in 2026 already appears optimistic. With inflation close to 1% in France, it will continue to be well below that of the euro area, where we expect inflation to average 1.9%.

However, investment decisions probably consider longer-term inflation expectations. In that regard, the priced-in inflation by the market for France over the next 10 years is only marginally below the eurozone's average market pricing of inflation.

Anyway, inflation differences between member states are normal within a monetary union and will not lead the ECB to change its stance, as long as there is no risk of an inflation undershoot for the whole of the eurozone. Monetary policy must be set for the euro area, not tailored to a single country.

Nevertheless, it is likely that the Governor of the Banque de France will continue to warn his counterparts about the risks of too low inflation. In particular, the issue of the euro’s appreciation and its impact on imported inflation will remain under scrutiny. We indeed believe that a further strong appreciation of the euro could reopen the door to interest rate cuts.

Low inflation not necessarily harmful to the economy…

It should also be recalled that low inflation in France is not intrinsically negative for the economy. François Villeroy de Galhau regularly stresses that more subdued inflation has allowed wage growth in France to remain more moderate than in partner countries, thereby supporting competitiveness.

Since the end of 2019, the increase in unit labour costs in market sectors has been 10 percentage points lower than in Germany, and 6 percentage points lower than in the euro area.

In theory, this competitiveness gain is an asset. However, it still needs to translate into faster growth. So far, political and fiscal uncertainty in recent months has prevented any significant rebound in activity. One can only hope that these competitive advantages will eventually feed through to growth in the months ahead.

A serious threat to public finances sustainability

I believe that where low inflation could become truly problematic is in the dynamics of public debt. Low inflation implies weak nominal growth. If, as we expect, real GDP growth stands at around 1% in 2026, with inflation also close to 1%, nominal growth would barely reach 2% (the GDP deflator is pretty close to consumer price inflation).

At the same time, according to the latest European Commission forecasts, the implicit interest rate on French public debt would rise to 2.2% in 2026 and 2.4% in 2027. In this context, nominal growth is no longer sufficient to offset the interest burden, and even in the absence of a deficit, the public debt-to-GDP ratio would mechanically increase (because debt to GDP is above 100%).

For France, the problem is even more acute as public finances are already in very poor shape. The 2026 budget plans for a public deficit of 5% of GDP, after 5.4% in 2025, which would push public debt to 118.2% of GDP, compared with 115.9% in 2025.

This compromise budget has resolved none of the structural imbalances. With the presidential election scheduled for April 2027 approaching, budget discussions for 2027 are expected to be even more delicate, making a lasting reduction in the deficit unlikely in the coming years.

Under these conditions, France risks remaining durably trapped in a high-deficit regime, while low inflation keeps nominal growth at an insufficient level. In the absence of a major positive productivity shock capable of sustainably lifting real growth, the public debt ratio will continue to deteriorate rapidly.

This deterioration will be more pronounced than in other European countries, such as Belgium, where the deficit is also high, but more dynamic inflation provides stronger support to nominal growth.

It is often said that inflation is the debtor’s friend. In the French case, its weakness acts as an accelerator of existing vulnerabilities. The sustainability of public finances, therefore, continues to stand out as one of France’s main macroeconomic challenges for the years ahead.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Crispr Therapeutics: Very High Risk But Even Higher Potential Reward
data.description:
Investment Thesis

GARP (Growth At a Reasonable Price) is by far my preferred strategy, strictly speaking about stocks, because options are much more complex and versatile. And while my stock portfolio is mostly focused on IT (hardware and software), I also look at other industries, for diversification. For example, in biotech I look for the following traits:

  • Either a company with a portfolio of products in an incipient phase to give it very high growth potential, and free cash flow positive. However, these are difficult to find, there are mostly some niche companies here.
  • Or a company with a recently launched product with blockbuster potential, with a cash position large enough to last until they become free cash flow profitable, and preferably debt free.
  • Or a company with a product in an advanced phase and with high chances of approval. I sometimes close these positions immediately after approval, or even just before approval if the stock advanced enough, depending on factors like fair value estimate, cash position, etc.

Therefore, I look for very high growth ahead, although I acknowledge that these profiles come with a high risk too. That's why I usually initiate small positions in about 10 names. Even if one of them crashes, my biotech sub-portfolio (which is just a small part of my entire portfolio) should still be good enough.

One of the companies that fits well in the second category is CRISPR Therapeutics AG (CRSP). It has a newly launched product in the gene-editing area, CASGEVY, which clearly has blockbuster potential. With ~$1.97B in cash & equivalents and no debt, they have a cash runway of at least 2-3 years. Then, for this category I usually look for companies with 2-3 products in their pipeline, just enough to offer them some sales continuity but not too many to deplete their cash reserves too fast. However, since it's difficult to find this combination, I sometimes look at companies with one launched product and no pipeline, other times at companies with one product and a more extended pipeline. CRISPR is in this last group, but I am content with their cash position (for now).

The next question is, of course: is the current price appealing enough to initiate a position? I will try to answer below.

Business Analysis and Market Estimates

CRISPR Therapeutics is a Swiss based company focused on developing gene-based medicines for serious human diseases. Like mentioned in the introduction, they have a broad and diversified pipeline, which I usually don't find a good idea for non-profitable companies relying on a single product. I generally prefer them to focus on 2-3 pipeline drugs until they become free cash flow positive, and then they can diversify. But let's see in more detail:

CASGEVY

CASGEVY is an ex-vivo gene-editing therapy, designed as a one-time treatment for patients aged 12 and older with severe SCD (sickle cell disease) or TDT (transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia), by using the CRISPR-Cas9 technology. It is jointly owned by Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) and CRISPR Therapeutics, with Vertex recording 100% of the revenue and costs, and subsequently sharing 40% of the net profits or losses with CRISPR.

Launched in 2024, CASGEVY accelerated meaningfully in 2025, with infusions occurring in all regions (US, EU, UK, Middle East) for both SCD and TDT:

The biggest hurdles for CASGEVY are its logistical complexity, safety concerns and high cost. The process involves extracting patient cells, shipping them to a specialized facility, editing them, and re-infusing them. The safety concerns are mostly related to a pre-conditioning chemotherapy phase, used to clear existing blood-forming stem cells from the bone marrow. However, the benefits of CASGEVY are generally considered to outweigh the risks of the associated chemotherapy, with 100% of SCD patients and 98% TDT patients achieving 12 consecutive months of freedom from hospital (with a mean duration of 36 months and 40 months, respectively) and with ~95% of patients free of vaso-oclusive crises for at least 12 months.

The list price is $2.2M in the US, which triggered some discussion about its "equitable access". There is a close price in Canada, C$2.8M (~$1.9M), with some requests to reduce it to C$1.7M. In the UK, the list price is GBP1.65M (~$2.1M), although there are some discounts. The official EU-wide pricing is not public, with confidential negotiated discounts applied in different European nations, but it's also expected to be close to $2M. With some normal discounts, I'll model an average of $1.5M per patient.

In the next step, I look at their potential market:

  • There are about 35000 plus 23000 patients considered immediately eligible (with severe forms of SCD and TDT).
  • There is potential for expansion into pediatric severe SCD and TDT, with good results presented in 2025 and regulatory submission expected in H1 2026.
  • TAM is significantly larger with in vivo and non-chemo conditioning (and I must say that this is a main reason for my interest), with Crispr's LNP (lipid nanoparticles) technology and Vertex actively working on a gentler conditioning, although just in research for now.

CASGEVY's main competitors are:

  • Bluebird Bio/Genetix: Lyfgenia (for SCD), although with a black box (and curiously enough, with a higher list price than that of CASGEVY), and Zynteglo (for TDT).
  • Beam Therapeutics: Risto-Cel (in development for SCD).

In conclusion, I model: Total 58000 potential patients (I ignore the expansion potential because it's too far away in time) x 50% market share for gene therapies (due to chemo-risks and insurance coverage) x 1/3 for CASGEVY x $1.5M average price x 40% for CRISPR = ~$5.8B for 2030.

However, CASGEVY has orphan drug exclusivity until 2031 in the US, and about the same in the EU. Other emergent players could enter the market in 2031, so I model halved revenues for 2031, followed by other modest decreases.

Pipeline

CTX611 is their most advanced candidate. This is a factor XI siRna, a long-acting anticoagulant that uses small interfering RNA to knock down the production of clotting Factor XI in the liver, aiming to prevent thrombosis without causing significant bleeding risks. It is in an ongoing Phase 2 clinical trial in patients undergoing TKA (total knee arthroplasty), but with multiple indications seen possible:

The thrombosis market size is estimated to reach ~$40B in 2031. Factor XI is a small piece of pie in 2025 but estimated to have ~8% CAGR. With intense competition either from other Factor XI or from diverse oral inhibitors, I see maybe between $500M - $2B potential peak sales x 30%-40% probability of success (potential launch in 2029) x 50%-50% with Sirius Therapeutics, for $150M peak sales.

CTX310 is an in vivo CRISPR/Cas9 gene-editing therapy designed to knock out the ANGPTL3 gene in the liver. Using LNPs (lipid nanoparticles) for delivery, it aims to durably lower triglycerides and LDL cholesterol to treat severe cardiovascular diseases:

It reached ~50% mean LDL and ~55% mean triglyceride reductions with well-tolerated safety profile in a Phase I basket study. The company sees a "muti-billion commercial opportunity", but with competition from Eli Lilly, which acquired Verve Therapeutics' similar program, let's round it to $1B. With 15% probability of success for a Phase 1 program (and potential launch in 2031), I model $150M peak sales.

Zugo-gel is an allogeneic CAR-T, which uses immune cells from healthy donors, in Phase 1 for SLE (Systemic Lupus Erythematosus) and other auto-immune diseases and Phase 1/2 in B-cell malignancies.

SLE has ~$2.2B market size, but this is a very competitive market. B-cell malignancies is an ~$3B market today but also with very intense competition. With other risks like unclear long-term effects (allogeneic therapies have historically struggled), severe side effects for ~17% of patients for its highest dosage, I pass, I don't model anything.

Fair Value

I find a relative valuation not suitable for CRSP, because it just started to generate sales from CASGEVY and because it's a unique, gene-editing company.

For an absolute valuation, I use a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model, and I start with my peak sales estimates described in the above section:

(million USD)

…2030

…2035

CASGEVY

Total Revenue

$2500M

Table 1: CRSP's Revenue Estimates (Author)

The other assumptions in my model are:

  • I model a local maximum of $350M for R&D expenses in 2028, one year before the estimated launch of CTX611, a local minimum of $325M in 2031, after the estimated launch of CTX310, then 6%-10% growth per year, with still a healthy pipeline, for 16.5% margin in 2035.
  • For "Collaboration" expenses, I model a CAGR of 30% through 2029 for the CASGEVY launch (and CTX611 but with 30%-40% probability), followed by modest growth for about ~29% margin in 2035. However, for CTX310 I will model Cost of Revenues, because this is only internally developed, with 20% terminal margin in 2035, in line with other biotech companies.
  • G&A expenses grew only marginally last year, but I do model a healthy 7% growth rate with more products in advanced phases.
  • Terminal tax rate in 2035: 24%, but this is just an estimate, it can vary with multiple jurisdictions and unclear fiscal policies.
  • Growth in perpetuity: I usually model 0% for small biotech companies, but here I will model 1.5%, coming either from in-vivo or non-chemo conditioning for CASGEVY, or from other candidates in pre-clinical stages.
  • Discount Rate: I usually model 12%-13% for a company relying on a single product, ~12% with an already launched product, and ~13% with a product waiting for approval, so I will model 12.25% in this case.

Resulting Fair Value is $91.95, about 73% potential upside from the current price ($53.07). For an analysis of sensitivity, let's further vary growth in perpetuity and discount rate:

Fair Value

Rate=13.5%

Rate=12.25%

Rate=11%

Growth=0%

$80.25

$88.08

$97.62

Growth=1.5%

$83.09

$91.95

$103

Growth=3%

$86.75

$97.06

$110.39

Table 2: CRSP Fair Value Estimate (Author)

We can observe the stock undervalued even with more modest assumptions: discount rate of 13.5% and zero growth in perpetuity.

However, I think that revenue is the variable with the biggest uncertainty in the model, so I want to do another exercise, a reverse DCF: what revenue inputs would be necessary to justify the current price? If I model a more modest growth to CASGEVY peak sales of $3B in 2030 (accompanied by slightly lower growth for "Collaboration" expenses), followed by a constant $1.6B from 2031 to 2035, then Fair Value would be $52.59, around the current price. This is not such an unlikely scenario (more about risks later).

Short-Term Signals

The options market can give us many times interesting hints. For example, an Open Interest Put-Call Ratio of 0.55 is considered bullish, because there more Call options than Put options:

This is truly a bullish signal, because the Call options are spread over multiple expiration dates and strike prices and are especially OTM (Out-of-The-Money). Even more, let's look under the hood at the Jan '27 expiration, where there are apparently many Put options too. But there are 2102 Puts for the $55 strike and 2239 for the $52.5 strike, so it's clear that this is, in fact, a Put Spread. If we ignore them, the Open Interest Put-Call ratio is ~0.46. And if we also consider the bullish Volume Put-Call Ratio of 0.23 (although these transactions came immediately after the last earnings report, so it's less likely to be from insiders), I consider this signal strong.

For a second signal, if we look at insider transactions, there are two purchases in July and August last year at $52.03 and $57.03, so around the current price, and especially the first one had a very large value. I consider this signal moderate, for a strong signal I would like to see more recent insider purchases.

Then, if we look at technical analysis, we can see the stock crossing its 50-day moving average upwards, which can sometimes be bullish, like it was in June-July or in September-October:

However, the stock was in a consolidation pattern since December, so this signal is not so clear, I consider it neutral.

Finally, CRSP's short interest is very high with ~25% of float shorted. However, please notice that this is a decreasing trend since July 2025, therefore I consider that these two signals offset each other. There is also a possibility of a short squeeze, but this is difficult to predict, we can see the stock going down from October until December despite a decreasing short interest. Therefore, I consider this signal neutral.

Risks

With this very high price, CASGEVY is dependent on insurance coverage. It seems that ~90% of US patients have reimbursed insurance access to treatment, and likely about the same in the EU, but things can change, especially in the US, after the expiration of its NTAP (New Technology Add-On Payment) status, while in other countries there are usually multi-year agreements. If we add that some patients might be reluctant to chemotherapy, that's why I included in my model only 50% of eligible patients for 2030.

I also talked about the price pressure, and not only in the US. While I modeled an average price of $1.5M, things can change until 2030, for example a multi-year contract can be negotiated, but at a lower price. The combination of these two factors (possible even less than 50% eligible patients treated plus a lower price) made me say that the reverse DCF (with $3B revenue in 2030 instead of $5.8B and $52.59 Fair Value) is not such an unlikely scenario.

Their cash reserves of about $1.97B should easily last for about two-three years. For example I modeled about $600M cash burn for 2026, and profitability by 2029. With higher sales in 2028, cash burn should be lower, therefore they have chances for enough cash runway, without needing to raise more capital. However, I acknowledge that both R&D and Collaboration expenses could come higher, so they might need more capital somewhere in 2028-2029. Especially if we look at consensus, they are estimated to become profitable only by 2030:

Finally, there are several risks related to their dependence on a single product. For example, Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) suffered a severe crash after a patient died after receiving its gene-editing treatment. While they use different approaches (Intellia's treatment is in-vivo) for different diseases, and while no death or secondary cancer has been associated with CASGEVY or its chemotherapy yet, this is a very serious risk, even if unlikely.

Then, like I said, I hope they could advance to an in-vivo or non-chemo gentler conditioning, but what if some competitors could come with such solution first? Some good results even in a more incipient phase, combined with a lack of results for VRTX/CRSP, could also dramatically affect CASGEVY. And if we add the eventuality of failure in CTX611 and CTX310, that could dramatically affect the company.

Strategy

For a GARP (Growth At a Reasonable Price) stock, I usually initiate a position when the sum Undervaluation + Revenue growth + Free Cash Flow yield is greater than Uncertainty Threshold.

For a company in such an incipient phase it's more difficult to choose revenue growth, because they start from a very low base (basically zero). Therefore, I will use my CAGR estimate only after 2026, which is ~57% through 2035.

For Uncertainty Threshold, I use the following values:

  • About 35% for a medium uncertainty
  • About 50% for a high uncertainty
  • About 75% for a very high uncertainty

I generally use a very high+ uncertainty for a company relying on a single product, or even extreme if that product is not approved yet. In this case, there is a rich pipeline, but this is more than offset by the risks associated with CASGEVY, especially since I noticed that reverse DCF scenario with not-shy chances. Therefore, I will use an uncertainty threshold of 90%.

With the sum 73% (undervaluation) + 57% (estimated revenue CAGR) - 12% (free cash flow yield, estimated for 2026) = 118%, CRSP is above my uncertainty threshold, therefore I rate it as "Buy".

However, with so many risks mentioned above, some of them severe, some with not-shy probability, I limit my position to ¼ or 1/3 maximum. But then, why am I still getting involved? Because, while I see very high risk, I see even higher potential reward. If they could come first with in-vivo / non-chemo conditioning, or if they report good results for their pipeline candidates, the stock could easily fly above $100 (perhaps helped by a short squeeze in such scenarios), and my initially small position will simply grow due to price appreciation.

In my personal portfolio, I opened a tiny ~0.12-0.13x position in spring 2024 (which grew to ~0.2x today due to price appreciation), and I am adding another 0.12-0.13x today. For my Seeking Alpha portfolio, since this is my first article about CRSP, I will just model 0.25x at today's price.

Takeaway

CASGEVY's gene-editing therapy targets severe SCD and TDT, and I model 2030 peak sales of $5.8B, based on market size, market share and price estimates. However, with orphan drug exclusivity only until 2031, I model $2.5B sales in 2035, with minor aids from pipeline candidates CTX611 and CTX310.

CRSP trades at ~$53, while my DCF-based fair value is $91.95, reflecting 73% potential upside, with undervaluation even under more conservative assumptions for cost of equity and growth in perpetuity. However, more conservative CASGEVY scenarios suggest CRSP might be just fairly valued.

There are multiple risks like CASGEVY's insurance coverage, pricing pressure, potential adverse CASGEVY effects especially related to chemotherapy, dependence on a single product, pipeline execution, and possible competitive threats.

I limit my position to 1/4 - 1/3, with very high+ uncertainty, but asymmetric reward if pipeline advances.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Index Outlook: Stocks Explode Despite War Rumors, A Bull Trap?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Markets are exploding higher in today's action, as was hinted by the rebounding action in stock benchmarks. Hope that some saw our previous day's analysis to catch the move!

The morning bullish action is closely linked to rebounds in Mag 7s that have been performing roughly since the beginning of 2026, and to financial equities attempting to shine again after rolling in the deep.

This shouts dip buying as investors seek opportunities while overall sentiment cools – traders are not forgetting the Warren Buffett adage: "Be fearful when others are greedy, and greedy when others are fearful."

However, in recent times, it's been tough to assess whether the market is actually fearful. Yes, the tech sector and anything related to AI are seeing bloodshed. However, indexes are still very near all-time highs, and asset managers are still overtly positioned, as expressed in the recent Bank of America survey.

The earnings season is a stellar one, so that would be a boon for stocks, but as markets are forward-looking, it is difficult not to show some skepticism about today's rebound.

Axios just published a piece that revives fear of an imminent war directed towards the Iranian regime, which keeps repressing its population amid the recent revolts, where more than 30,000 victims have been reported. To a large extent, war would definitely not help equities race towards new all-time highs.

Taking a step back, the Trump Administration is quite aggressive and opportunistic. So if there is an occasion for the US to trample one of its most opposing regimes at a time of weakness, it would be surprising to see nothing happening.

As I have been warning for a while now, this is a story to monitor for traders. Hence, traders would need to be careful about upcoming headlines, as the second warship is expected to reach the Middle East soon.

So is today's rebound a bull trap? It could be early to say, but this could offer a decent timing for risk-off opportunities. In the meantime, let's dive into today’s session charts and key trading levels for the major US indices: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow bounced sharply after forming a higher low yesterday. However, the index wicked at the 49,900 resistance and is seeing rejection at its 4H 50 MA (49,850).

Hence, with the narrative potentially turning to a risk-off, current levels could offer very decent entry levels for bearish positions. Any close above 50,000 would invalidate this thesis.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • 49,900 to 50,000 resistance (session highs and rejection)
  • 4H 50 MA: 49,850
  • Intraday resistance 50,250 (rejecting)
  • ATH resistance 50,400 to 50,500
  • Index all-time highs: 50,512

Support Levels

  • Major support – 49,000 (breaking this should lead to further downside)
  • Past week support 48,600 to 48,700
  • Key support around 47,500
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Now reaching the key 25,000, Nasdaq is at a very interesting technical point. This level acted as resistance after the October 2025 crash and could act similarly if sentiment turns frothy.

Any break above 25,250 would void the setup, while a session close below 25,000 would confirm a downturn.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Key pivot: 25,000 to 25,250
  • 25,400 to 25,500 key intraday resistance
  • Pivotal resistance 25,700 to 25,850
  • 26,246 FOMC highs
  • All-time high resistance zone 26,100 to 26,300

Support Levels

  • 24,500 to 25,600 key support (bearish below)
  • February 5 lows: 24,165
  • October-November support: 23,800 to 24,000
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

As mentioned in our previous day's analysis, the S&P 500 went to retest its key 4H 50 and 200-period moving averages and looks to be rejecting them. Reaching a 6,911 high this morning, profit-taking occurred right ahead of the 200 MA, which gives a first sign of weakness.

Rejecting below the pivot zone (closing below 6,900 on the session) should confirm the bearish turn. Any break back above 6,920 voids the bearish outlook.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • 4H 50 MA 6,900 (immediate rejection)
  • Session top: 6,911
  • Previous ATH resistance 6,945 to 6,975
  • Current ATH: 7,020
  • All-time high resistance: 7,000 to 7,020 (range highs)

Support Levels

  • Mini support: 6,830 to 6,850 (bearish below)
  • 6,800 psychological support
  • Overnight lows 6,700 (range lows)
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades and keep a close eye on the US-Iran developments!
